response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I'll be honest with you—I don't spend much time on social media myself, which probably puts me in a better position than most to talk about this problem. I've seen how it affects people around me, though, and it genuinely troubles me. My wife scrolls through Facebook more than I'd like, and I worry about what she's absorbing without really thinking about it. When you're working with your hands all day like I do, you don't have time for the nonsense that floods those platforms, but that doesn't mean I'm blind to what's happening out there.

The way I see it, dealing with fake content comes down to the same principle I apply to my carpentry work: you've got to inspect your materials before you use them. Just like I wouldn't build someone's deck with warped lumber, you shouldn't trust information that hasn't been properly vetted. The problem is that most people don't have the patience or the skepticism to do that checking. They see something that confirms what they already believe, and they share it without a second thought.

I've learned to be cautious about what I read, even in the limited time I spend online. When I see something that seems too outrageous or perfectly aligned with my own biases, I pause. I ask myself whether I'm seeing this because it's true or because an algorithm decided I'd be more likely to engage with it. That anxiety I naturally carry—I try to channel it into healthy skepticism rather than letting it fuel panic about whatever story is going viral that week.

What I tell my friends and family is this: slow down. Don't share something immediately just because it feels important or makes you angry. Check the source. See if reputable news outlets are reporting the same thing. Ask yourself who benefits from you believing this particular story. It takes a few extra minutes, but it's worth it. And honestly, if you're anything like me and you worry about these things, you might actually feel better knowing you're not contributing to the problem. That's something concrete you can control, which is more than most people can say about social media these days.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 44-year-old carpenter with no social media presence, married with one child, living in Washington DC. His high neuroticism (79) suggests he's naturally anxious and concerned about problems, while his low conscientiousness (1) indicates he doesn't follow strict routines or detailed protocols. His moderate openness (39) means he's somewhat traditional in thinking but not completely closed off. His vocational education and working-class background inform his practical, hands-on approach to problem-solving. His lack of social media means he views this topic from an outside perspective, giving him a unique vantage point. His hobbies like volunteering and photography suggest he values authenticity and careful observation. His Christian faith might inform values around truth and integrity. The tone should be practical, slightly anxious but grounded, and reflective of someone who thinks in concrete terms rather than abstractions.
